text.front:
Received: 19 July 2023
Revised: 5 March 2024
Accepted: 29 April 2024
IET Renewable Power Generation
DOI: 10.1049/rpg2.13014
ORIGINAL RESEARCH
Experimental investigation of BIPV/T application in winter
season under Şanlıurfa's meteorological conditions
Yusuf Can Demir 1
Mehmet Azmi Aktacir 2
1 Construction Works and Technical Department,
Kocaeli University, Umuttepe Campus, İzmit, Turkey
2 Department of Mechanical Engineering,
Engineering Faculty, Harran University, Osmanbey
Campus, Haliliye, Turkey
Correspondence
Yusuf Can Demir, Construction Works and
Technical Department, Kocaeli University,
Umuttepe Campus, İzmit, 41001, Turkey.
Email: yusufcan.demir@kocaeli.edu.tr;
yusuf.91@hotmail.com
Funding information
Scientific Research Projects unit of Harran
University, Grant/Award Number: 22187; Kocaeli
University, TUBITAK ULAKBIM
Abstract
Considering that nearly 39% of the total CO 2 emission released into the atmosphere
today are thought to be due to the energy consumed by buildings, the importance of
taking measures through buildings to combat global warming is evident. Therefore, the
concept of nearly zero-energy buildings (NZEB) is come to the forefront. Building inte-
grated photovoltaic thermal (BIPV/T) systems are used to enable buildings to generate
their own energy. However, buildings have limited facade and roof areas required for
BIPV/T systems. Therefore, in this study, various configurations of bifacial (double-sided)
and monofacial (single-sided) panels were compared to investigate ways to enhance the
efficiency of BIPV/T systems. Different air flow velocities and varying air gap distances
were tested for both panel types. By placing a reflective surface on the wall behind the bifa-
cial panel, the electrical efficiency of the bifacial panel was increased and proven through
PVsyst analysis. Both panels provided maximum heat efficiency at the shortest air gap dis-
tance under high air flow conditions. In addition, it was shown in both the experimental
setup and Comsol CFD analysis that it provides significant benefit in the thermal energy
load of the building when heating the interior environment in winter. In terms of electrical
power production surplus, the bifacial panel outperformed the monofacial panel in all con-
figurations, with a minimum advantage of 8.33% and a maximum of 12.73%. Additionally,
the maximum electrical efficiency was obtained from the bifacial panel in configurations
with the longest air gap distance. Using the bifacial panel in the BIPV/T system with the
shortest air gap distance during the heating season and the longest air gap distance during
other seasons can provide the highest efficiency for the building throughout the year.
1
text.body:
INTRODUCTION
1.1
Background
Due to the use of fossil fuels, the increasing greenhouse gas
emissions are causing global warming to rise day by day [1].
It is known that in developing or emerging countries, nearly
40% of total energy consumption is consumed by buildings
[2]. Therefore, there is an increasing trend towards renew-
able energy sources and nearly zero-energy buildings (NZEB).
Among renewable energy sources, solar energy was one of the
most heavily invested sources in recent years [3]. Therefore,
there is a growing interest in building integrated photovoltaic
thermal (BIPV/T) systems in industrial and residential buildings
[4].
text.front:
This is an open access article under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is
properly cited.
© 2024 The Author(s). IET Renewable Power Generation published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd on behalf of The Institution of Engineering and Technology.
text.body:
Solar radiation absorbed by PV cells but not converted into
electricity increases the temperature of the PV cells, which in
turn reduces their efficiency [5]. To overcome this problem,
air or water is used as a cooling fluid in PV panels [6]. Cooling
fluids are delivered to the panels through the integration of
thermal collectors with various designs. When the heated panel
comes into contact with the cooling fluid inside the thermal
collector, heat transfer occurs from the panel to the fluid. As
a result, cooling the panel increases its electrical efficiency, and
at the same time, hot air or water is obtained from its waste
heat. These systems are called Photovoltaic Thermal (PV/T)
systems [7]. Globally, in 2023, the PV/T collector area reached
1.5 million m 2 , equivalent to a thermal capacity of 789 MW and
an electrical capacity of 276 MWp [8]. This PV/T system, when
integrated into buildings and used by the building for both
text.note: DEMIR and AKTACIR
text.body:
electricity and thermal energy, is referred to as the BIPV/T
system [9]. Due to being relatively new emerging systems
worldwide, the use of BIPV and BIPV/T systems continues
to increase annually. In terms of energy, globally, solar thermal
systems provided approximately a total of 440 TWh of heat
in 2022, while photovoltaic systems supplied 1312 TWh of
electricity [8].
BIPV/T is a high-efficiency solar energy system with the
capability of producing hot air/water and electricity, aimed at
effectively meeting the energy demand of the building [10]. It
supports the electricity consumption needed in buildings. In
BIPV/T systems, the cooling fluid is used not only for cool-
ing the PV panel but also for the purpose of heat collection.
Especially in winter, it is used for heating the indoor spaces of
buildings, and in summer, it is used for hot water supply and
drying processes [11]. In addition, passive heating and lighting
of the interior using semi-transparent panels are among the pri-
mary benefits of the BIPV/T system. Thus, it minimizes the
utility bills for both electricity and building heating, leading to
economic savings.
Since BIPV/T systems utilize energy from the sun, they
can be used continuously. In addition, while producing energy,
it does not emit any gases to the environment like fossil
fuels, making it environmentally friendly. The CO 2 emissions
resulting from the energy consumed by buildings make up
approximately 39% of the total emissions [12]. When consider-
ing such a high rate of CO 2 emissions in buildings, the transition
to safe and sustainable energy production systems like BIPV/T
is being encouraged [13].
BIPV/T systems can make significant contributions to envi-
ronmental considerations in the context of sustainable energy
solutions. By producing clean and renewable energy, it reduces
dependency on fossil fuel-based energy production and it can
reduce greenhouse gas emissions by up to 1/3 in both devel-
oped and developing countries. Additionally, up to 40% of
the global energy demand in these countries can be met with
BIPV/T. This can lead to reducing the carbon footprint orig-
inating from buildings to 0 [2]. BIPV/T panels might have a
lower environmental impact compared to other types of solar
panels. Because they are integrated into building structural ele-
ments, there's no need for additional land coverage, and they
contribute to the preservation of soil resources [14]. Since
BIPV/T systems can be integrated into the facades or roofs
of buildings, they can enhance the aesthetics of the buildings.
It allows buildings to meet their own energy needs, offering
the opportunity for on-site energy production. For this rea-
son, it reduces the need for long energy transmission lines
and encourages the more efficient use of energy resources
[15]. In conclusion, BIPV/T systems significantly support envi-
ronmental considerations in the context of sustainable energy
solutions. However, factors such as material selection, produc-
tion processes, waste management, and operation/maintenance
practices need to be taken into consideration. In this way,
these systems can both enhance energy efficiency and support
environmental sustainability [16].
BIPV/T systems provide on-site electricity and heat energy
to buildings. However, they are typically mounted on the facade
or roof of the building, so they do not cover a large area. It is
essential for them to provide high energy efficiency in a small
area. Especially in locations where there are plenty of sunny
days, particularly during the winter season, higher efficiencies
can be achieved annually from this system. The aim of this
study was to investigate the parameters influencing the design
for increasing the energy production per unit area of BIPV/T
systems.
1.2
Literature review
In the literature, various methods have been employed to
increase the efficiency of PV/T and BIPV/T systems. Among
these methods are the use of air or water at different flow
rates, variable panel transparencies, different air gap depths,
installation under various climate conditions, and different panel
technologies. Pantic et al. [17] indicated that with the increase
of fan speed, electrical energy also increases, and suggested an
optimal fan speed. According to the study, when wind speed
increased from 1 to 7 m/s, electrical efficiency increased by
1%, while thermal efficiency decreased by 6%. In their study,
Gül and Akyüz [18] found that in air-based PV/T (photo-
voltaic/thermal) systems with cooling applied, there was a
12.9% increase in electricity production compared to the case
without cooling. Teo et al. [19] demonstrated that the cooling
effect of an air-based PV/T system was enhanced by attach-
ing fins to the back of the PV panel, resulting in improved
PV performance. In another similar study, conducted by Saygın
et al. [20], the size of the air channel and airflow rate used to
cool the panel surface were optimized to increase the electrical
efficiency. Mojumder et al. [21] found the highest thermal effi-
ciency of 56.19% in a high-fin arrangement under maximum
flow and irradiance settings. It was shown by M. Hajji et al.
[22] that in the PVT system, the panel temperature decreases
and its performance increases with an increasing water flow
rate. According to the research conducted by Tina et al. [23],
the BIPV system using ventilated bifacial panels provided 2.9%
more power compared to the ventilated conventional BIPV sys-
tem, and 4.4% more power compared to the non-ventilated
conventional BIPV system. In the study conducted by Wajs
et al. [24], it was observed that by passing air behind the PV
panel mounted on a tile frame, there was an increase of 9.5%
in electrical efficiency and 18.3% in thermal efficiency. In the
study conducted by Kabul and Duran [25], it was found that
the maximum electrical power in the PV/T panel, where water
is passed as the fluid for cooling, is 35% higher compared
to the panel where no cooling is applied. In the experiment
conducted by Cuce and Cuce [26], it was determined using
fluent CFD that the PV power output increased with cooling
applications, and the most effective PV cooling configuration
was found to be at an air velocity of 1 m/s and 15 • C air
temperature.
Another parameter that increases electrical efficiency is the
transparency of the panel material. Kamthania et al. [27] com-
pared opaque and semi-transparent panels in a double-pass
BIPV/T system and found that due to the heat retention
text.note: 17521424, 2024, 9-10, Downloaded from https://ietresearch.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1049/rpg2.13014 by Cochrane Portugal, Wiley Online Library on [08/09/2025]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License
DEMIR and AKTACIR
text.body:
property of opaque panels, semi-transparent panels have a
higher electrical performance. Yang and Athienitis [28] studied
BIPV/T systems with opaque and semi-transparent PV panels
and found that the thermal efficiency of the semi-transparent
panel was 7.6% higher.
Among the factors that affect electrical and thermal effi-
ciency, the depth of the channel distance from the back surface
of the panel to the other surface also plays a significant role.
Xu et al. [10] found that in cases with 57 and 25 mm channel
depths of PVs, the inlet and outlet air temperature difference
was formed in the case with a maximum channel depth of
25 mm, and the thermal efficiency increased as the channel
depth decreased. In the study conducted by Kaiser et al. [29],
it was found that in a natural ventilation scenario of an air-based
PV/T system, the cell temperature reaches its minimum when
the channel depth/channel length ratio is 0.11. In addition, a
19% increase in power output was observed at air velocities
ranging from 0.5 to 6 m/s. In their study, Kim et al. [30] exam-
ined the effect of air flow rate and channel size on the thermal
efficiency of a PV/T panel. The study showed that along with a
22% thermal efficiency of the system, there was a 5 • C increase
in air temperature.
The efficiencies of BIPV/T systems can vary depending on
climatic conditions. For example, Bot et al. [31] demonstrated
that a BIPV/T system exposed to the temperate environment
in Portugal had a thermal efficiency ranging from 3.3% to
4% and an electrical efficiency of 15.1%. In addition, in the
BIPV/T study conducted by Xu et al. [10] in Beijing, Hefei,
and Xining, the annual total energy savings in the three cities
were measured as 2661.8, 1908.4, and 2412.3 kWh, respectively.
Kamel and Fung [32] combined an air-source heat pump with
a BIPV/T system to create a highly efficient heating system
for Canada's cold climate and achieved an annual savings of
500 dollars. Luo et al. [33] found that in China, the PV/T-air-
water facade system had an average daily efficiency of 7.8%
for the PV/T-water mode during the summer months, with a
total energy production of 973 kJ. During the transition period,
the efficiency increased to 11.6%, resulting in 3226.4 kJ of
total energy production. The system's energy production and
efficiency increased from summer to winter. Shao et al. [34]
demonstrated that in the city of Dalian, China, the summer
maximum electrical, thermal, and overall efficiencies of the mul-
ticrystalline silicon hybrid BIPV/T system were 11.22%, 111%,
and 86%, respectively. In winter, these efficiencies decreased to
10.58%, 81%, and 58%, respectively.
PV panel technology is one of the parameters that affect
the efficiencies of BIPV/T systems. Aaditya et al. [35] stud-
ied amorphous silicon (a-Si) and mono-crystalline silicon (m-Si)
for the BIPV/T system. According to the analyses, while a-Si
exhibited better performance, m-Si provided higher electrical
efficiency. Urbanetz et al. [36] compared a BIPV with a-Si
technology to a curved PV system and noted that a-Si has a
higher annual energy gain. In the study by Pereira and Aelenei
[6], phase-changing gypsum board material was added to the
wall part of the BIPV/T system, and this system was analysed.
Up to 64% maximum thermal efficiency was achieved in win-
ter tests, while up to 32% efficiency was obtained in summer
tests.
1.3
Contribution of the research
In the current literature, it was observed that there are more
studies on PV/T systems compared to those on BIPV/T
systems. This indicates potential research gaps for BIPV/T sys-
tems and presents opportunities for future research [37]. In
the literature, monofacial (single-sided) panels are generally pre-
ferred to increase the thermal and electrical efficiencies of PV/T
and BIPV/T systems. No BIPV/T study comparing bifacial and
monofacial panels under the same conditions were encountered
in the literature.
This study offers an innovative perspective by exploring the
potential of bifacial panels to meet the thermal and electrical
energy needs of buildings in Şanlıurfa, Turkey, under cold cli-
mate conditions. The innovation in the study is the comparison
of monofacial and bifacial panels with the same electrical pro-
duction capacity under equal conditions in the BIPV/T system.
The most notable innovation of the study is placed a reflective
surface on the building wall behind the bifacial panel to enhance
production from its rear side. In addition, different air gap dis-
tances and air speeds were used for both panels. Given that the
study was conducted on the experimental building setup during
winter conditions, another innovation of the research is deter-
mining the degree of improvement in the indoor temperature
compared to the outdoor temperature.
This study provides valuable insights into the potential
advantages of bifacial panels for residential and industrial build-
ings in Şanlıurfa by comparing the performance of a single-sided
PV panel with that of a bifacial PV panel under the same
conditions.
1.4
Structure of the paper
BIPV/T system was installed on the southern facade of the
examined building, and the effectiveness of structural and
physical parameters such as panel surface temperature, air tem-
perature, panel technology, ventilation, which have an impact
on the electrical and thermal performance of photovoltaic pan-
els, was experimentally investigated. In the experiment, both
bifacial and monofacial photovoltaic panels were tested. An alu-
minium foil was placed as a reflector on the surface of the
wall behind the bifacial glass-glass photovoltaic panel. Energy
efficiency is compared with the monofacial PV panel with
the same potential placed next to this panel. In addition, the
effect of air cooling by passing airflow through the intervals
between the wall and the panel using a fan was determined
on energy efficiency. The effectiveness of using this airflow
in space heating was determined in terms of its impact on
the efficiency of the heating system. The experimental setup
was compared with real data by simulating it in the PVSYST
program [38].

FIGURE 1 (a) Exterior view of BIPV/T system design. (b) Interior view. BIPV/T, Building integrated photovoltaic thermal

2
MATERIAL AND METHODS
2.1
Experimental design and setup
The aim of this study was to investigate the BIPV/T appli-
cation under the meteorological conditions of Şanlıurfa. For
this purpose, the experimental setup was established at Harran
University GAP-YENEV Research and Application Center. In
this study, the container shown in Figure 1a,b was used. Vari-
ous characteristics of this structure are provided in Table 1. A
BIPV/T system was created by placing one bifacial and one
monofacial PV panel on the southern facade of this structure
(Figure 1a). The position of the fans that provide air movement
inside and the air vents are shown in Figure 1b.
The technical characteristics of the monofacial and bifacial
panels used in the experimental setup are presented in Table 2.
EPS, Expanded polistyren foam. PVC Polyvinyl chloride.
The cross-sectional view of the experimental BIPV/T model
examined in this study is illustrated in Figure 2. The front
surfaces of the panels were mounted vertically relative to the
ground and facing south on the wall of the container. The inci-
dent solar radiation on the front surface will generate electrical
energy from the PV panels and increase the surface tempera-
ture. The air taken from the indoor environment is directed to
the back of the PV panel with the help of a fan, and the heated
air is then expelled back into the indoor environment through
the upper air vent. The back wall of the bifacial BIPV/T sys-

tem is covered with aluminium foil, which enhances its reflective
properties. An interval is left between the PV panel and the
back wall, and a transparent tarpaulin is installed between the
PV panel and the wall to prevent air leakage.
Three different intervals, measuring 5.5, 8.5, and 11.5 cm, are
left between the wall and the PV panel. Thus, in the BIPV/T
application, three different intervals were achieved using the
sliding mechanism shown in Figure 3.
The energy generated by the panels used in the BIPV/T
application is sent to the micro inverter used in the system. The
connection was made with the wiring shown in green colour in
Figure 4. The direct current (DC) reaching the micro inverter
was converted to alternating current (AC) and connected to the
grid through the electrical cable (shown in orange colour in the
diagram). The energy generated by the PV panel is being mea-
sured instantly in the micro inverter. The data flow from the
micro inverter is transferred to the cloud system every 15 min
text.note: 17521424, 2024, 9-10, Downloaded from https://ietresearch.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1049/rpg2.13014 by Cochrane Portugal, Wiley Online Library on [08/09/2025]. text.body:
FIGURE 2 Cross-sectional representation of the BIPV/T system design for the bifacial and monofacial panel. BIPV/T, Building integrated photovoltaic
thermal.
FIGURE 3 Demonstration of the design of the sliding mechanism
apparatus drawn in SolidWorks.
as watts through the data transfer unit (DTU). Access to this
information is possible through the application provided by
the manufacturer of the inverter, either via mobile devices or
web-based platforms. The technical specifications of the micro
inverter and DTU are provided in Table 3.
The points measured in the experimental study are schemat-
ically shown in Figure 4. Here, the ends of the thermocouple
cables, which are placed at ten different points for tempera-
ture measurement, are indicated in yellow colour. Data logger
connections were made with the thermocouples, and measure-
ments were taken every 10 min. In addition, the solar radiation
intensity was measured using a pyranometer placed on the south
facade and recorded every 10 min on data logger. The techni-
cal specifications of the pyranometer and data logger device are
provided in Table 4.
To obtain temperature measurements from various points of
the container, 10 T-type thermocouple cables were used. The
temperature of the air entering the air duct from the indoor
environment was measured using thermocouple cables num-
bered 1 and 2, located in the lower part of the air channels
on the southern facade. For temperature measurements num-
bered 3 and 4, the cables were mounted respectively on the
exit side of the air channels of bifacial and monofacial pho-
tovoltaic panels. The objective at these points is to determine
the temperature difference by measuring the temperature of the
air entering the lower channel with cables numbered 1 and 2
and the temperature of the air when it enters the indoor envi-
ronment after being heated in the channel. The 5th and 6th
temperature measurement points are, respectively, the bifacial
panel and the monofacial panel. Termocouple cables for tem-
perature measurements on the panels were attached to the back
of the panels using special adhesive tape. The seventh and eighth
temperature measurements were attached to the walls behind
the bifacial panel and the monofacial panel, respectively, in order
to measure the temperatures of the walls (Figure 4).
At points 9 and 10, the temperatures of the outdoor and
indoor environments were measured. The temperature of the
indoor environment was measured using a thermocouple cable
placed on the inner ceiling of the container. In order to measure
the outdoor temperature accurately and from a location shielded
from direct sunlight, the thermocouple probe was placed inside
a shade apparatus (Figure 5).
The holes with a diameter of 15.2 cm were punched into the
walls corresponding to the bottom and top sections of the pan-
els in order to prepare the air channels. The 15 cm diameter flex
pipes were connected to the lower holes by applying silicone,
forming a seal. A fan is connected to each of the other ends of
the flex pipes to ensure air circulation (Figure 1b). The power
consumed by the fans was measured using a wattmeter. When
the fans were operating at low speed, the average air velocity
was measured as 9.8 m/s, the average volumetric flow rate was
found to be 278 m 3 /h, and the power consumption was mea-
sured as 32 W. When the fans were operating at high speed,
the average air velocity was measured as 12.9 m/s, the aver-
age volumetric flow rate was found to be 366 m 3 /h, and the
power consumption was measured as 57 W. Figure 6 shows a
photograph of the experimental setup used in this study.

2.2
Conducting the experiment
This study covers experiments conducted over a total of 9 days
in February 2023, from 9:00 in the morning to 17:00 in the
evening, to determine the effectiveness of BIPV/T application
during the heating season (winter) under the meteorological
conditions of Şanlıurfa. To better demonstrate the effective-
ness of the BIPV/T system and to increase indoor temperature
during the winter season, experiments were conducted on the
following sunny days as specified below.
In the experimental setup, the photovoltaic panels used were
tested on a sliding system at three different intervals from the
wall and under three different air velocities. The fan speeds were
0.4 m/s (natural air flow), 9.8 m/s (low fan speed), and 12.9 m/s
(high fan speed).
The BIPV/T configuration and experimental parameters
investigated in this study are presented in Table 5.
text.note:
17521424, 2024, 9-10, Downloaded from https://ietresearch.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1049/rpg2.13014 by Cochrane Portugal, Wiley Online Library on [08/09/2025]. text.body:
FIGURE 7 Flowchart of equations used in calculation methods.
2.3
Calculation method
In this section, the calculation procedures related to the data
obtained from the experimental measurements were described.
Here, calculations related to the thermal energy and efficiency
as well as electrical energy and efficiency of the BIPV/T system
are given.
To determine the heat energy transferred to the indoor envi-
ronment, it is necessary to find the mass flow rate of the air 𝑚
(kg/s) and Q˙a ir (kJ/s) values. For this, the formula for the volu-
metric flow rate of air was given in Equation (1) on the flowchart
in Figure 7. In the experimental setup, the volumetric flow rates
measured with an anemometer are 0.101 m 3 /s when the fans
are operating at high speed, 0.077 m 3 /s when the fans are oper-
ating at low speed, and 0.00314 m 3 /s in the fanless condition
[39]. In Equation (2,) the ρ value represents the density of air,
and it has been taken as 1.1839 kg/m 3 in this study. The val-
ues V (m/s) and A (m 2 ) represent the air velocity and the air
hole area, respectively. Their multiplication gives the previously
calculated volumetric flow rate. The product of the volumetric
flow rate Ʋ (m 3 /s) and the density ρ (kg/s) equals the mass flow
rate
.
m (kg/s) [39].
After calculating the mass flow rate of the air, the equations
given in (3) and (4) were used to determine the thermal power
and energy gained from the air, respectively.
Equation (3) is used to calculate the thermal energy gained by
the air inside the air duct (refer to Figure 2). In this equation, T o
( • C) represents the temperature of the air entering the channel
cavity of the photovoltaic panel from the lower hole. T 1 ( • C)
represents the temperature of the air entering the indoor envi-
ronment from the upper hole, which increases as a result of the
solar radiation in the channel cavity. Another parameter used in
this equation is c p (kJ/kg • C), which represents the specific heat
capacity of air at constant pressure. This value is c p = 1,0057
(kJ/(kg • C)), is valid for air between -100 • C and +100 • C
[39]. These calculations were made for both photovoltaic
panels.
In addition to the thermal power determined by Equation
(3), the total energy produced in various timeframes such as
daily, monthly, etc., is calculated in kWh using Equation (4) in
the study. The h value in Equation (4) represents the duration of
the operation in hours (Figure 7).
In general, the thermal efficiency (𝜂 t ) of photovoltaic pan-
els is calculated using Equation (5). The Q˙(kW) value here is
the thermal power calculated in Equation (3). I T is the average
amount of solar radiation per day per unit area and is the unit
W/m 2 . A represents the panel area, and its unit is m 2 . Both
panel areas are approximately 1.67 m 2 .
In determining the electrical power of photovoltaic pan-
els, the product of the instantaneous current (in amperes)
and voltage (in volts) gives the power value (P) in watts
(W). In this study, the power values are directly measured
using microinverters. Similarly, the electrical energy values
(kWh) for the operating durations are also determined using
Equation (6).
In Equations (6) and (7), the P (kW) value represents the
electrical power. The electrical power is measured by the micro
inverter in the experimental setup and converted to energy unit.
Equation (7) is used to determine the electrical efficiency. I T is
the average daily amount of solar radiation per unit area and is
the unit W/m 2 . A represents the panel area, and its unit is m 2
(Figure 7).

text.body:
FIGURE 8 Experimental setup installation in PVsyst program. PVsyst, Photovoltaic software.
2.4
PVsyst simulation study and Comsol
CFD analysis
In this study, the investigated BIPV/T model was created in the
PVsyst program, as shown in Figure 8, and a 1-year simulation
was conducted.
The photovoltaic panels selected for this study are monofa-
cial and bifacial panels with a power rating of 335 W. The area
where the panels are placed was drawn in the program based
on the dimensions of the container, leaving ≈9 cm interval
between the panel and the container wall. In order to simulate
the meteorological conditions specific to Şanlıurfa, the map of
the program was marked with the location of the experimen-
tal study at Harran University GAPYENEV Center. Hoymiles
brand MI-500 microinverters with a power rating of 500 W
were selected for each panel. Both panels were set to have a tilt
angle of 90 • and an azimuth angle of 0 • , ensuring that they face
directly south. Since the panels are positioned vertically, their
surfaces do not accumulate dust. Therefore, a pollution rate of
0% was assumed in the program.
The monthly and annual production values obtained from
the program were compared with the results obtained in the
experimental study to analyse and compare the production
values.
In addition to the analysis of electrical energy with PVsyst,
the temperature distribution of the configuration conducted on
February 24th has been thoroughly examined using the Com-
sol CFD simulation program. The study was conducted as
three-dimensional modelling. Therefore, the upper part of the
structure (+z axis) was defined with sunlight, reflecting the con-
tinuously changing weather conditions as it occurs in the real
world. The sun was set to rise and set between the -x and +x
axes, from east to west. The Comsol mesh module was preferred
for mesh distribution, and a total of 118,504 tetrahedral meshes
were generated (Figure 9). The PARDISO solver was utilized
as a solution method for high performance, robustness, multi-
processor memory, and efficient utilization (Sogukpinar, 2019;
Pardiso, 2022). To eliminate the domain size effect on the com-
putational results, the computational domain size was simulated
by expending the air portion by ten times the length of each
edge of the model.
3
RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
Figure 10 presents the experimental results obtained on Febru-
ary 16th. The experiment was conducted at a distance of first
(5.5 cm) with the fans operating at high speed. The solar radia-
tion intensity was 509 W/m 2 at 9:00 am and reached its peak at
12:00 am with a value of 827 W/m 2 . With this increase, the tem-
perature of the panels, panel walls, and indoor environment also
increased. However, the maximum temperature was obtained
at the highest air temperature points. While the air tempera-
ture was 8.14 • C at 13:40, the temperature of the bifacial panel
was 37.1 • C and the temperature of the monofacial panel was
41.1 • C. Although the temperature of the monofacial panel is
higher, the temperature of its back wall is lower compared to
the temperature of the back wall behind the bifacial panel. Due
to the bifacial panel's glass-glass and transparent construction,
it allows for easier transfer of the temperature formed on its sur-
face to its backside. Consequently, it was observed that the use
of glass-glass bifacial panels in BIPV/T systems reduces electri-
cal efficiency losses due to temperature compared to monofacial
panels. In addition, the external temperature was measured as
8.14 • C while the internal temperature was measured as 26 • C.
When the fans were operating at high speed during the time
when the internal and external temperatures were at their high-
est, an approximate temperature gain of 18 • C was achieved in
the internal environment. This indicates that the BIPV/T sys-
tem is a suitable heating system for buildings and can result in
fuel cost savings during winter.
Measurements were taken for nine different configura-
tions as detailed in Table 5 in the experimental setup.
In Figure 11, daily average radiation and daily average

FIGURE 9 Mesh distribution on the container structure.
FIGURE 10 Radiation and temperature data obtained during the experimental setup on 16 February 2023.
text.note: 17521424, 2024, 9-10, Downloaded from https://ietresearch.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1049/rpg2.13014 by Cochrane Portugal, Wiley Online Library on [08/09/2025]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License
DEMIR and AKTACIR
text.body:
FIGURE 11 Data of daily irradiance and temperature averages taken in nine different configurations.
temperature values for all experimental configurations are
provided.
The daily average temperature distribution obtained from the
indoor ambient temperature during the days of the experiments
was determined to range between 20.5 and 33 • C. The daily aver-
age outdoor temperature during the same period varies between
6.7 and 18 • C. The temperature difference between the indoor
and outdoor temperatures was found to be between 12 and
15 • C. As previously mentioned, this showed that the BIPV/T
system can support the heating system.
In the experiments conducted on February 16, 17 and 20, in
which the fan worked at high speed, the amount of radiation
decreased day by day respectively. Despite this, the tempera-
tures in the two panels and their walls gradually increased toward
February 20. As the panels moved away from the wall, the circu-
lated air reduced the cooling effect on the panel. The panel was
found to achieve the best cooling when it was positioned at a
distance of 5.5 cm from the wall. In addition, it was determined
that the cooling performance is best when the mass flow rate of
the air is 0.1196 kg/s, which corresponds to the fan operating at
high speed. As a result, it can be concluded that the amount of
heat energy supplied to the indoor environment is most effec-
tive when the panels are positioned at a distance of 5.5 cm. In
the experiment conducted by Sopian et al. [40], it was found that
in an air-based double-pass PV/T system with a 5 cm air inter-
val, increasing the flow rate between 0.018-0.04 kg/s resulted
in an increase in both electrical and thermal efficiency. Fur-
thermore, Sopian's study found that increasing the mass flow
rate significantly contributed to the cooling of the panel. On
February 21, 22, and 23 (Figure 11), similarly, despite a decrease
in solar radiation intensity, the panel temperatures decreased
to a lesser extent. During the mentioned dates, when the fans
were operating at low speed (0.0912 kg/s), the panels were best
cooled when the distance between the panels and the wall was
5.5 cm. As the distance of the air interval increased, the cooling
effect of the panels by the air decreased.
On February 24, 25, and 26, when the panels were cooled
with natural air flow (0.0037 kg/s) without fans, the panel
temperature increased significantly compared to the parame-
ters with forced convection. In the study conducted by Cuce
and Cuce [26], it was mentioned that the temperature of the
PV cell decreased with increasing air velocity at a constant
air temperature. As shown in Figure 12, the increase in the
temperature of the PV panel has slightly reduced the electric-
ity generation compared to the incident solar radiation. In the
BIPV/T study conducted by Wajs et al. [41], under a solar radi-
ation equal to 900 W/m 2 , the maximum operating temperature
of the PV panel reached 79.6 • C. With cooling, the tempera-
ture was reduced to 72.2 • C. Approximately 9% more power
was obtained from the PV panel compared to the uncooled
condition. In our BIPV/T study, it was observed that, in the
case of high fan speed, the bifacial panel and the monofacial
text.note:
17521424, 2024, 9-10, Downloaded from https://ietresearch.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1049/rpg2.13014 by Cochrane Portugal, Wiley Online Library on [08/09/2025]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License
DEMIR and AKTACIR
text.body:
FIGURE 12 Data on daily radiation and power generation averages taken in nine configurations.
panel achieved 7.8% and 6.9% higher efficiency, respectively,
compared to the situation without cooling.
During the natural convection (fanless) configuration, espe-
cially the temperature of the bifacial panel was observed to
be higher than that of the monofacial panel (Figure 11). In
cases where cooling is performed by forced convection, tem-
peratures of monofacial panel have exceeded temperatures of
bifacial panel. Due to the fact that bifacial panels are made
of glass-glass and are semitransparent, sunlight passes to their
backside. In the absence of cooling, the solar radiation pass-
ing to behind the panel heats the panel from both sides. This
also causes the bifacial panel to heat up more than the non-
translucent monofacial panel. In addition, it was observed that
the thermal transmittance of the glass-glass panel (0.78 W/mK)
is higher compared to the monofacial panel with polyethylene
terephthalate (0.29 W/mK). Therefore, as a result of increased
air velocity, it was found that the cooling effect on the bifacial
panel is greater compared to the monofacial panel (Figure 11).
As seen in Table 7, an increase in wind speed at the 1st distance
has resulted in a greater improvement in both thermal and elec-
trical efficiency in the bifacial panel compared to the monofacial
panel. The use of materials with high thermal conductivity in
BIPV/T systems ensures an increase in efficiency.
The indoor and outdoor air temperatures generally changed
proportionally to each other, as observed in Figure 11. On
February 26th, while the external air temperature averaged
18 • C, the internal temperature, on average, was measured at
33 • C, which was the highest indoor temperature recorded dur-
ing the experiment. Since the internal volume of the container
is 11.3 m 3 and the container is covered with heat-insulated
polystyrene foam with a density of 14 kg/m 3 and a thickness of
5 cm, it can be said that the indoor temperatures in the experi-
ment are high in terms of human comfort. However, when this
thermal gain is installed in larger offices or rooms, it is thought
that the indoor temperature may be at a level suitable for human
comfort. On February 16th, the average outdoor temperature
was 6.7 • C, while the average indoor temperature was found
to be 20.6 • C. It shows that the BIPV/T system will provide
a significant gain in heating load in winter for indoor heating.
As seen in Figure 11, the temperature differences between the
temperature of the air entering the air duct from the bottom and
the air leaving the top are at the lowest level when the fans are
operating at high speed. As the air interval distance increases,
the temperature difference between the upper and lower air
ducts decreases in general. Thanks to the fan, the air sent from
the lower hole absorbs a certain amount of heat from the panel
and increases its temperature by passing from the upper side to
the indoor environment. However, the faster the air rises, the
smaller the temperature difference was observed. The highest
temperature difference was observed when the fans were not in
operation, as indicated by the graph. Because the air slowly heats
up and rises, it has the opportunity to absorb heat over time. In
addition, since there is no forced convection, the temperature
of the panel increases more significantly. However, this thermal
gain in natural convection cannot immediately heat sudden air
cooling in the room. In the case of opening any door or win-
dow, when the fans are operational, they are able to restore the
indoor temperature to its previous level within a few seconds.
When examining the days on which the experiments were
conducted, it can be observed that the amount of incoming radi-
ation is directly proportional to the generated power (Figure 12).
The most important result here is that the amount of power
produced by the bifacial panel is higher than the monofacial
panel.
In Table 6, the amount of extra power produced by the bifa-
cial panel compared to the monofacial panel, according to the
days of the experiments, is shown as a percentage. The bifa-
cial panel, overall, achieved higher production compared to the
monofacial panel. The semitransparent nature of the bifacial
panel allowed the sunlight to pass through to the backside,
resulting in the reflection from the aluminium foil. This further
increased the electricity generation on the back surface of the
panel. As the panels moved away from the wall, the production
rate of the bifacial panel increases more than the monofacial
panel. As the panels move away from the wall, the increase in
the radiation entering from the transparent tarpaulin next to
the panels and the effect of the semi-transparency of the bifa-
cial panel caused the bifacial panel to produce better electrical
energy than the monofacial panel. When evaluated compara-
tively, it was observed that in the configuration where the panels
are positioned farthest from the wall and the fans are operat-
ing at high speed, the bifacial panel achieved the best electricity
text.note: 17521424, 2024, 9-10, Downloaded from https://ietresearch.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1049/rpg2.13014 by Cochrane Portugal, Wiley Online Library on [08/09/2025]. generation with a rate of 12.73% compared to the monofacial
panel. The parameter that resulted in the lowest production was
achieved with a rate of 8.33% in the configuration performed
on February 24th. The use of the new technology of glass-
glass bifacial panels in Building integrated photovoltaic/thermal
(BIPV/T) systems was found to be significantly better in terms
of electrical energy efficiency compared to traditional panels.
In Figures 13, 14, and 15, current-voltage (I-V) curves for all
configurations conducted in the experimental setup were gener-
ated using data obtained from the data transfer unit (DTU). In
all configurations measured from 9:00 AM to 5:00 PM, the volt-
age values of the panels connected to the same micro-inverter
were found to be nearly identical. Table 6 compares the power
output of bifacial panels to monofacial panels, demonstrating
that bifacial panels produce a higher amount of power. The
reason for the higher power output of bifacial panels is the
differentiation in current values. In Figures 13-15, the current
values of the green curve representing the bifacial panel were
found to be higher in each configuration compared to the blue
curve representing the monofacial panel. This situation indi-
cates that the use of the back surface of the bifacial panel has not
affected the voltage values. In each configuration, it was found
that the reason for the higher power output of the bifacial panel
compared to the monofacial panel is due to the higher current
values.
Furthermore, it was observed that the current and volt-
age values are inversely proportional when examining the I-V
graphs of each of the nine configurations. Current values tend
to increase during times of strong solar radiation and decrease
during weak solar radiation, while voltage values tend to rise.
As shown in Table 7, the electrical and thermal efficiencies of
PV panels vary depending on the air velocity and the distance of
the panel from the wall. In general, it was observed that when
the fans are operating at high speed, the panels achieve higher
electrical energy efficiency compared to the situation where the
fans are operating at low speed, at the same distance from the
panels. The decrease in the temperature of PV panels results in
an increase in their electrical efficiency. In the air-based BIPV/T
experiment conducted by Pantic et al. [17], it was found that
increasing the air velocity resulted in an increase in electricity
generation. Teo et al. [19] tried to cool an air-based PVT sys-
tem by installing fins. In this study, with a temperature of 70 • C,
the electrical efficiency was 9%. Electrical efficiency increased
to 13% when the panel temperature was reduced to 35 • C by
means of air and fins.
When examining Table 7, it was found that the electrical effi-
ciency of the bifacial panel increases as it moves away from the
wall. Thanks to the reflective surface behind the bifacial panel,
it was observed that the rear surface is more effectively utilized
as it moves away from the wall. The thermal efficiency is higher
when the gap distance between the panel and the wall decreases,
as this allows for better contact of the passing air with the sur-
face and increases heat transfer. As the air gap distance between
the panel and the wall widens, both panels showed a tendency
of decreasing thermal efficiency. In addition, it was determined
that in fanless configurations, the thermal efficiency of mono-
facial panels is higher compared to bifacial panels. The absence

text.body:
FIGURE 16 The amount of energy produced by the bifacial panel and
the monofacial panel, respectively, in February (data from DTU). DTU, Data
transfer unit.
of a fast airflow resulted in the glass-glass bifacial panel retain-
ing more heat. This situation can be substantiated by the graph
in Figure 11, where it is observed that the temperature of the
bifacial panel is higher than that of the monofacial panel.
According to the study of Karthich and Catherine [42], it was
observed that PVT lowered the panel temperature compared to
PV and increased the efficiency of the whole system. Compared
to this study, both thermal efficiency and electrical efficiency
were found to be lower in both panels in Table 5 in all fanless
configurations than in forced convection configurations. Sim-
ilar to the aforementioned study, both thermal efficiency and
electrical efficiency were found to be lower in both panels in
all fanless configurations in Table 7 than in forced convection
configurations.
Based on the data obtained from the experimental setup,
in February, the bifacial panel produced 35.75 kWh of energy,
while the monofacial panel produced 32.20 kWh (Figure 16). In
addition to daily average values, when examining monthly elec-
trical energy productions, the bifacial panel has approximately
11% more production than the monofacial panel.
To see the monthly and yearly electrical energy gain of the
bifacial panel compared to the monofacial panel, and to vali-
date the February production from the experimental setup, the
PVsyst analysis results was provided in Table 8. In the experi-
mental setup, the electricity production value for the monofacial
panel was found to be 32.2 kWh in February. When com-
pared with experimental data, there is a margin of error of
0.9% in monofacial panel values in Pvsyst. The results were
found to be consistent with each other. This result demonstrates
the accuracy of the model used in the simulation compared
to the experimental outcomes. In addition, an analysis of the
bifacial panel was conducted using the PVsyst program. How-
ever, due to limitations in the simulation program, it was not
possible to define the reflective surface placed on the wall
behind the bifacial panel in the experimental setup, and as a
result, the use of the reflective surface was not incorporated in
the analyses. Therefore, in the energy production comparisons
between the experimental data and the simulation for the bifa-
cial panel in February, it was found that the experimental data
was 7.5% higher. Thus, by comparing the experimental setup
with the PVsyst analysis, the reflective surface is proven to be
an important parameter.
In addition to this, in the PVsyst analysis, the bifacial
panel produced 3.6% more electrical energy than the mono-
facial panel in February. However, in the experimental setup
for February, the bifacial panel has an 11% advantage over
the monofacial panel (Figure 16). From this perspective, it
was observed that, similarly, the reflective surface used in the
experimental setup enhances the efficiency of the bifacial panel.
When examining the annual total energy production in the
PVsyst analysis, it is observed that the bifacial panel pro-
duced 421 kWh of energy, while the monofacial panel produced
401.88 kWh. According to the PVsyst analysis, the bifacial panel
generated an additional 4.76% electrical energy annually com-
pared to the monofacial panel. Based on this, when looking
at the electrical energy values produced by the panels for all
months, the superiority of the bifacial panel is proven each time.
In addition, it is estimated that the bifacial panel will produce
approximately 446 kWh of electrical energy annually when using
a reflective surface.
In the PVsyst analysis, as can be seen from the graphic
results in Figure 17, the total loss of monthly electrical energy
was higher in the monofacial panel than in the bifacial panel.
The total loss rates shown in purple on the graph are lower
on a monthly basis due to the higher efficiency of the bifa-
cial panel. In addition, the usable energy ratio generated each
month (brown bar graph) is also higher in the bifacial panel.
The reason for the high system losses of the bifacial panel is
related to its energy production rate. The bifacial panel, which
shares the same infrastructure system as the monofacial panel,
has higher energy production, and as a result, its system losses
are also higher.
CFD analysis results were given to enable the comparison
of the temperature distribution on the container structure for
the configuration conducted on February 24th. The surface
temperature distribution of the container structure is given in
Figure 18. The temperature range varies between 15 and 55 • C.
When examining the temperature distribution, it is observed
that the highest temperatures are in the PV panels. This situation
has proven the extraction of thermal energy from the panels in
the BIPV/T system during winter.
Under the realistic radiation value of 749 W/m 2 given at
12:00, the temperature distribution of the container structure
varies between 10 and 55 • C, as shown in Figure 19b. Addition-
ally, when the time-dependent temperature graph in Figure 19a
is examined, it can be observed that there is a similarity between
experimental and numerical results. This situation indicates that
the measurements obtained from experimental data are consis-
tent with the results of numerical simulation, confirming the
mutual validation of both methods.
The temperature distributions of monofacial and bifacial
panels at 12:00 under 749 W/m 2 solar irradiance are pro-
vided in Figures 20b and 21b, respectively. It is observed
that at this time, the surface temperature of the monofacial
panel varies between 52 and 54 • C, while the surface tem-
perature of the bifacial panel varies between 52 and 55.5 • C.
The fact that the bifacial panel has a higher panel tempera-
ture than the monofacial panel in the fanless condition is also
substantiated by the average temperature graph provided in

FIGURE 18 Temperature distribution of the container structure in the
CFD simulation program. CFD, Computational fluid dynamics.
Figure 11 above. When examining the time-dependent tempera-
ture graph in Figure 20a, it is observed that the numerical results
are higher in the morning, lower around noon, and closer to
the experimental results in the afternoon. This situation illus-
trates the variation in the panel's performance throughout the
day. The numerical model demonstrated an accurate simula-
tion of the panel's behaviour, especially during the afternoon
hours.
When examining the temperature graph in Figure 21a,
it is observed that the numerical results are closer to the
experimental results in the morning and around noon, but
lower in the afternoon. This situation demonstrates that the
numerical model successfully simulates the temperature varia-
tion of the photovoltaic (PV) panel, especially during specific
time intervals. When the experimental data is compared with
the CFD analysis results for both panels, it is generally
observed that they are compatible with each other, as shown in
Figures 19-21.
FIGURE 19 (a) Time-dependent experimental and numerical results of the internal environment of the container structure. (b) The temperature distribution
of the internal environment at 12:00.
4
CONCLUSION
In this study, the BIPV/T system was made more efficient with
new technologies and various parameters. The electrical effi-
ciency of the bifacial panel was found to be higher in every
configuration compared to the monofacial panel. In the exper-
imental setup, the placement of a reflective surface on the wall
behind the bifacial panel was resulted in higher electrical pro-
duction compared to a traditional bifacial panel. This situation
was substantiated by comparing the results of the experimental
setup and PVsyst analysis. In the winter, there was not a con-
sistent advantage in the thermal efficiency of the bifacial panel
in different configurations. However, when we consider that
we do not use thermal energy in buildings during the winter
months, the superiority of the bifacial panel over the monofa-
cial panel in total energy throughout the year is indisputable.
Furthermore, increasing the gap distance between the wall and
the bifacial panel to enhance the albedo effect allows the bifa-
cial panel to achieve a higher production rate. It enables higher
efficiency per unit area to be achieved from limited spaces on
building facades. Thus, it provides more support in meeting the
building's energy load. The costs are nearly the same as mono-
facial panels. Therefore, the payback period is also shorter. In
addition, when transparent glass-glass bifacial panels are used
in buildings, savings can also be achieved in lighting expenses.
At night, electric power can be generated as a result of the
reflection of indoor lighting used on the back of the bifacial
panel.
As seen from the findings in the experimental setup, it is
evident that the thermal and electrical efficiency of the bifa-
cial panel in BIPV/T systems has been increased by adding
a reflective surface and using forced convection (fan-assisted
heat transfer). It was observed that the thermal efficiency of
the bifacial panel reaches significantly higher levels when placed
closest to the wall with forced convection. Furthermore, it
was observed that the electrical efficiency of the bifacial panel
reaches high levels when it is placed at the farthest distance
text.note:
17521424, 2024, 9-10, Downloaded from https://ietresearch.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1049/rpg2.13014 by Cochrane Portugal, Wiley Online Library on [08/09/2025]. text.body:
FIGURE 20 (a) Time-dependent experimental and numerical results of the monofacial panel. (b) Temperature distribution of the monofacial panel at 12:00.
FIGURE 21 (a) Time-dependent experimental and numerical results of the bifacial panel. (b) Temperature distribution of the bifacial panel at 12:00.
from the wall. Therefore, during the heating season, maintain-
ing a short air gap distance in the BIPV/T system for the
bifacial panel and increasing it during other seasons can sup-
port an approach aimed at maximizing energy intake from the
panel throughout the year. Taking this factor into account in
building designs can help reduce the building's energy load and
contribute to achieving nearly zero-energy buildings (NZEB).
Analysing the building's energy consumption requirements can
help determine the areas where the BIPV/T system will be
effective. In building designs, it is essential to first measure
the solar energy potential of the region using simulation tools.
BIPV/T systems can be integrated as part of the building, cov-
ering the roof or facade with the most extended exposure to
sunlight. The generated electrical energy should be integrated
into the building's electrical system. An energy management sys-
tem can be used to reduce unnecessary electricity consumption.
Alternatively, electricity generated by the BIPV/T system can
be sold to generate income using a bidirectional meter. For the
efficient utilization of the heat energy produced by the BIPV/T
system, it can be integrated into the automation system to work
in commutative with the building's heating system. During the
day, when the panels reach a certain temperature, the automa-
tion system can automatically shut off the building's heating
system. Under the control of the automation system, the heat
generated by the BIPV/T system can be directed to the indoor
space using fans, thus saving energy from the building's heating
system. The initial investment cost of the BIPV/T system can
be recovered in the long term with the right energy management
strategies.
Both BIPV/T systems, which can be utilized in both residen-
tial and industrial sectors, and the evolving panel technology are
regarded as a significant step towards reducing the consump-
tion of fossil fuels and attaining a sustainable environment by
mitigating the threat of greenhouse gases to the world.

of IET Renewable Power Generation journal. Thanks to
Aykut GÜZEL for their assistance with CFD simulation. The
experimental setup in this study was supported by the Sci-
entific Research Projects unit of Harran University. (Project
no: 22187). Institution/Funder Name: Kocaeli University,
TUBITAK ULAKBIM.
